title: Ablation Studies
concept: An ablation study changes one component at a time to test what actually matters, so teams can separate real gains from lucky mixtures of many simultaneous tweaks.

exercises.prompt: What is the simplest reason to remove one model component at a time during testing?

options.text: To see which part is actually helping.
To make every experiment more confusing.
To prove all components are equally important.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Ablation is about finding which ingredient earns its place.

cards.title: Do not praise the whole recipe too early
cards.body: A team adds data augmentation, label smoothing, and a new layer, and accuracy goes up. Nice. But which change helped? Without isolating the parts, the team cannot tell whether all three mattered or only one did the heavy lifting.

cards.title: What an ablation study does
cards.body: An ablation study starts from a model or training recipe and then removes or changes one component at a time. By keeping the rest fixed, you get clearer evidence about whether that single component helped, hurt, or barely mattered.

cards.title: Taste the soup after changing one ingredient
cards.body: If soup tastes better after you change the salt, herbs, cream, and stock all at once, you still do not know what fixed it. A careful cook changes one ingredient, tastes again, and learns something. Ablation studies use that same discipline.

cards.title: Testing the value of augmentation
cards.body: Suppose a medical-image model uses flips, color jitter, and a new attention block. An ablation might remove only color jitter while leaving everything else alone. If performance barely changes, that trick may not deserve as much credit as the team assumed.

cards.title: More tricks do not automatically mean more understanding
cards.body: A common myth is that if the final model is stronger, every added idea must have contributed. Not necessarily. Some changes may be redundant, neutral, or even slightly harmful while other changes carry the improvement.

cards.title: Piled-up changes vs. one-at-a-time testing
cards.body: Ablation studies trade speed for clarity, but that clarity is what makes conclusions trustworthy.

left.text:
Final score may improve.
Hard to know which idea mattered.
Future decisions stay fuzzy.

right.tag: One component tested at a time
right.text:
Each component gets evidence.
Useful ideas stand out clearly.
Weaker ideas can be removed.

exercises.prompt: If you want to know whether dropout helped, what is the best ablation move?

options.text: Keep everything else the same and remove dropout.
Change dropout, batch size, and optimizer together.
Compare this week's model with an unrelated model from last month.
Skip evaluation and assume dropout helped.

exercises.explain: Right. Ablation works by isolating one component while holding the rest steady.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An ablation study is useful even when the full system already performs well.
exercises.explain: True. Good performance does not tell you which ingredients were necessary.

exercises.sentence: Ablation studies help answer the question, “Which part actually ___?”
exercises.explain: Mattered is correct. The goal is to identify which change earned its place.

exercises.prompt: Which experiment sounds most like a clean ablation?

options.text: Train the same setup again but remove only the new augmentation policy.
Compare a CNN on cats with a transformer on medical scans.
Change four features and describe the result as proof for all four.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A clean ablation isolates one change at a time.

exercises.prompt: A paper says, “Our final system uses six improvements and beats the baseline,” but never shows what happens when each improvement is removed. What is missing?

options.text: An ablation study showing which components actually contributed.
A promise that every component must have worked equally well.
A rule that simple baselines are illegal.
A requirement to stop measuring validation performance.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Without ablations, it is harder to know which ideas truly earned credit.
